skill.name: incident-response-smart-fix
skill.description: Multi-agent orchestration workflow for systematic incident diagnosis and resolution using AI-assisted debugging, observability platforms, and automated testing.
skill.summary: A comprehensive incident response workflow that coordinates specialized AI agents through a five-phase debugging pipeline: issue analysis, root cause investigation, fix implementation, verification, and prevention.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analyzer detected 62 patterns but all are FALSE POSITIVES. The skill consists entirely of Markdown documentation files (.md) describing incident response workflows. Patterns flagged as 'external commands' are bash code blocks in documentation, not executable code. 'Windows SAM database' and 'weak crypto' references appear in example output templates, not actual implementations. No executable code, network calls, or file system operations present.

content.user_title: Resolve incidents with multi-agent AI orchestration
content.value_statement: Production incidents require coordinated investigation across multiple systems and domains. This workflow orchestrates specialized AI agents through a proven five-phase pipeline to diagnose root causes, implement fixes, and prevent recurrence.
actual_capabilities: Coordinate specialized AI agents (debugger, code-reviewer, test-automator, performance-engineer) through structured incident response phases
Analyze error traces, logs, and observability data from Sentry, DataDog, and OpenTelemetry
Automate git bisect to identify commits that introduced regressions
Route issues to domain-specific agents (Python, TypeScript, Go, Rust, database, security)
Generate comprehensive test suites including unit, integration, and regression tests
Implement prevention measures including static analysis rules, type enhancements, and monitoring alerts
limitations: Requires access to observability platforms (Sentry, DataDog) and version control for full effectiveness
Complex multi-system incidents require manual coordination between agent phases
Domain-specific fixes depend on having appropriate specialist agents available
Workflow assumes standard CI/CD infrastructure for testing and deployment verification

use_cases.title: Production error investigation and fix
use_cases.description: Use when production errors are impacting users and require rapid diagnosis and resolution. The workflow analyzes error signatures, identifies root cause through git bisect and code analysis, implements a fix with tests, and verifies no regressions before deployment.
use_cases.target_user: On-call engineers and SREs handling production incidents

use_cases.title: Cross-system performance degradation
use_cases.description: Use when performance issues span multiple services or layers (database, application, cache). The workflow coordinates database optimizers, performance engineers, and DevOps specialists to identify bottlenecks and implement optimizations with monitoring.

use_cases.title: Security vulnerability remediation
use_cases.description: Use when security scans identify vulnerabilities requiring code changes. The workflow routes to security specialists for fix implementation, adds security tests, performs penetration testing validation, and documents security improvements.

prompt_templates.title: Quick bug fix with basic testing
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this error and implement a fix: [paste error message]. Run basic tests to verify the fix works. Focus on resolving the immediate issue with minimal changes.
prompt_templates.scenario: Low-risk bugs, cosmetic issues, or documentation fixes requiring fast resolution

prompt_templates.title: Standard incident response with full verification
prompt_templates.prompt: Investigate this production incident: [describe symptoms]. Follow the four-phase workflow: (1) analyze error traces and logs, (2) identify root cause with git bisect and code analysis, (3) implement fix with comprehensive tests, (4) run regression suite and performance validation. Include rollback plan.
prompt_templates.scenario: Most production bugs, feature issues, and data bugs requiring thorough verification

prompt_templates.title: High-severity incident with prevention measures
prompt_templates.prompt: Respond to this critical incident: [describe impact]. Execute the complete five-phase workflow including long-term prevention. Add static analysis rules, type system enhancements, monitoring alerts, and create a postmortem. Configure canary deployment with success metrics and abort criteria.
prompt_templates.scenario: Security issues, performance problems, data corruption, or high-traffic system failures

prompt_templates.title: Multi-domain coordination for complex issues
prompt_templates.prompt: Orchestrate resolution for this cross-system issue: [describe systems involved]. Coordinate agents in sequence: [list agents]. Pass explicit context between phases including completed work, key findings, and remaining tasks. Verify integration points and end-to-end behavior.
prompt_templates.scenario: Issues spanning multiple services, teams, or technology domains requiring coordinated resolution

output_examples.input: Users experiencing timeout errors on checkout page with 500+ errors per hour
output_examples.output: Root cause identified: Missing database index on user_id column causing 5-second query times. Fix implemented: Added index reducing query time to 50ms, added Redis caching for user profiles. Tests: 24 unit tests, 8 integration tests, all passing. Monitoring: Alerts configured for query p95 latency and cache hit rate. Deployment: Canary rollout to 5% traffic with abort criteria defined.

output_examples.input: TypeError Cannot read property map of undefined affecting Safari iOS 14 users
output_examples.output: Root cause identified: API returns null instead of empty array when no results. Fix implemented: Added null checks and type guards in frontend, updated backend to return empty array per API contract. Tests: Cross-browser test suite passing including iOS 14 Safari. Prevention: TypeScript strict null checks enabled, OpenAPI spec updated to document array return type.

best_practices: Always identify root cause before implementing fixes - use git bisect and observability data to understand the failure mechanism, not just symptoms
Implement prevention measures for high-severity incidents - add static analysis rules, type enhancements, and monitoring to detect similar issues early
Document rollback plans and success metrics before deployment - define clear abort criteria and monitor key metrics during canary rollout
anti_patterns: Fixing symptoms without understanding root cause - this leads to recurring issues and technical debt
Skipping verification phases for speed - inadequate testing causes regressions and extends mean time to recovery
Implementing fixes without prevention measures - the same vulnerability pattern will reappear in other code locations

faq.question: How do I choose the appropriate verification level?
faq.answer: Use minimal for low-risk changes like documentation or cosmetic fixes. Use standard for most production bugs. Use comprehensive for security issues, performance problems, or high-impact incidents affecting revenue or many users.

faq.question: What if the issue spans multiple technology domains?
faq.answer: Use the multi-domain coordination pattern. Sequence specialist agents (for example: database-optimizer then performance-engineer then devops-troubleshooter) with explicit context passing between each phase using the context passing template.

faq.question: How do I handle issues in languages without a specialist agent?
faq.answer: Route to the general debugger and code-reviewer agents for analysis. For implementation, use available agents with similar paradigms or implement manually following the fix design provided by the review phase.

faq.question: Can this workflow handle incidents requiring immediate action?
faq.answer: Yes, use the minimal verification level and immediate rollout strategy for hotfixes. Focus on quick mitigation first, then follow up with comprehensive analysis and prevention measures once the immediate impact is resolved.

faq.question: What observability data do I need to provide?
faq.answer: Ideally provide error traces from Sentry or similar, relevant logs with correlation IDs, distributed traces from OpenTelemetry or Jaeger, and APM metrics showing latency and error rate trends. More data enables faster and more accurate root cause identification.

faq.question: How do I know when the incident is fully resolved?
faq.answer: An incident is resolved when: all tests pass with zero regressions, performance benchmarks are within acceptable thresholds, monitoring shows error rates returned to baseline, rollback plan is documented, and prevention measures are in place to detect similar issues.
